cards.front: What is a breakpoint in debugging?
cards.back: A marker you set on a line of code that tells the debugger to pause program execution when that line is reached.

cards.text: Setting a breakpoint allows you to {{c1::run}} the program normally until the breakpoint is hit, then {{c2::pause}} execution to inspect state.

cards.front: What happens when a breakpoint is hit?
cards.back: The program pauses execution BEFORE executing that line. You can then inspect variables, examine the call stack, and step through code.

cards.text: The {{c1::Run}} or {{c2::Continue}} command tells the debugger to execute normally until the next {{c3::breakpoint}} or program end.

cards.front: What's the difference between Run and Continue in a debugger?
cards.back:
Run: Start debugging from the beginning
Continue: Resume execution from current paused position

Both execute until next breakpoint or program end.

cards.text: You can set {{c1::multiple}} breakpoints in a program, and the debugger will pause at {{c2::each}} one as it's encountered.

cards.explanation:
Set breakpoints at strategic locations:
  • Start of suspect function
  • Before suspected problem code
  • In conditional branches to see which executes
  • In loops (especially first iteration)
  • After function calls that might fail
examples: // Set in if-block to verify it executes

cards.front: How do you typically set a breakpoint in an IDE?
cards.back: Click in the left margin next to the line number, or press F9 (typical shortcut) on the desired line.
A breakpoint that only pauses execution when a specified condition is true (e.g., when x > 100). Available in most modern debuggers.

cards.explanation:
Common debugging workflow:
  1. Set breakpoint where you suspect problem
  2. Run program in debug mode
  3. Program pauses at breakpoint
  4. Inspect variables and program state
  5. Step through code or continue to next breakpoint
  6. Identify issue and fix
  7. Remove breakpoints and retest
examples: // Set breakpoint at start of calculate()

cards.text: Breakpoints are {{c1::non-intrusive}} - they don't require modifying your {{c2::source code}} and can be easily enabled or disabled.

cards.front: Why are breakpoints better than stepping from the start of main()?
cards.back: Breakpoints let you run at full speed to the problem area, instead of slowly stepping through hundreds of lines of working code first.

cards.text: A breakpoint causes execution to {{c1::pause}} {{c2::before}} executing the marked line, allowing you to inspect program state.

cards.question: Where should you set a breakpoint to debug this crash?
cards.answer: Set breakpoint at the start of process() function or on the int* p = nullptr; line to inspect the pointer value before the crash.
cards.explanation: Setting the breakpoint before the crash lets you examine why ptr is null.
